personality_voice.book_baseline: In the book corpus, Neville is timid, accident-prone, and anxious but not without social awareness. He speaks in short, hesitant phrases ('You'd better open it, Ron... it'll be worse if you don't'), draws on personal experience to advise others (his own Howler from Gran), and reacts to danger with cowering and physical comedy (seized by pixies, swinging from chandelier, wand slipping in McGonagall's class). He is awestruck easily ('Amazing'), and his fear is consistent and observable. He also shows a degree of self-awareness about his own incompetence ('They went for Filch first... everyone knows I'm almost a Squib'). His voice is timid but not mute — he volunteers information and advice when he has it.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core traits: Neville faints at the sight of the Mandrake, draws back pale at the pixie cage, is seized by pixies and dropped onto Lockhart's desk, and delivers the line 'Why is it always me?' — a moment of resigned self-awareness. He advises Ron to open the Howler ('Go on, Ron. I ignored one from my Gran once... and it was horrible'), mirroring the book almost verbatim. He also speaks up in the common room scene ('Haven't any of the teachers noticed that the Slytherins are all safe?'), showing a flash of frustrated logic. He alerts Harry to the ransacked dormitory ('Harry, I don't know who did it, but... you'd better come').
personality_voice.difference: The film preserves the main personality markers well. The Howler advice, the fainting, the pixie victimhood, and the dormitory alert are all present. The film loses some texture: the book's detail about Neville buying protective talismans out of fear (despite being pure-blood), his self-conscious worry about being 'almost a Squib', and his tongue-poking confusion over subject choices are absent. These are minor secondary traits. The common room speech ('Haven't any of the teachers noticed...') is actually a film addition not present in this book corpus, but it is consistent with his character and gives him a small moment of agency.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main personality — timid, accident-prone, self-deprecating, occasionally perceptive — is preserved. The loss of the talisman-buying scene and the 'almost a Squib' self-identification removes some texture around his specific fear of being a magical failure, but these are secondary. Minor simplification warrants a small deduction.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In the book corpus, Neville functions primarily as comic relief and as a background presence that grounds the dormitory social world. He has small but real moments of agency: advising Ron about the Howler (initiating a course of action), alerting Harry to the ransacked room, and serving as a recurring victim of magical mishaps that illustrate the dangers of the wizarding world. He is also used as a social barometer — his fear of the Chamber attacks, his talisman-buying, and his reaction to Harry's Parseltongue all reflect the broader student mood.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves his advisory role with the Howler, his role as the dormitory witness who alerts Harry, and his victimhood in the pixie scene. The common room speech adds a small moment of initiative. He appears briefly in the Parseltongue aftermath (eyes 'glittering with fear'), maintaining his barometer function.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The talisman-buying scene — which in the book gives Neville a small independent action and reveals his specific fear — is cut. The book's detail of Neville's wand slipping in McGonagall's class (a recurring incompetence beat) is present in the book corpus but not in the film corpus. These are minor losses. His core functions are retained.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: His role is reduced but recognizable. No major agency is transferred to another character. The talisman scene is the most notable cut, as it gave him an independent action and a specific fear. Small deduction for compression.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: The book corpus does not give Neville deep explicit motivations or internal conflict in the scenes provided. His fear is consistent and observable. The closest thing to internal conflict is his self-identification as 'almost a Squib' — a fear of magical inadequacy that drives his anxiety and his talisman-buying. This is a meaningful motivational thread: he fears being magically insufficient and therefore vulnerable.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film does not explicitly address the 'almost a Squib' fear. His anxiety is present but attributed generically to danger rather than to a specific fear of magical inadequacy. The fainting and cowering are preserved but without the self-aware articulation of why he is especially afraid.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The specific motivation of fearing magical inadequacy ('everyone knows I'm almost a Squib') is absent from the film corpus. His fear reads as generic rather than rooted in a specific self-perception.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: This is a simplification of motivation rather than a replacement. The fear is still present; its specific root is not articulated. Given the limited corpus and Neville's minor role, this is a moderate but not severe loss. Confidence is low because the corpus provides limited motivational material for either version.

character_arc.book_baseline: Within this corpus (CoS only), Neville has no meaningful arc. He is a static background character — consistently fearful, accident-prone, and socially present. His arc across the full series is significant, but within this corpus it is flat by design.
character_arc.film_portrayal: Similarly flat within the film corpus. No arc is introduced or removed.
character_arc.difference: No arc exists in either version within this corpus. Both are flat.
character_arc.penalty_logic: No penalty. A flat arc in the books that is flat in the films scores full points per the rules. However, confidence is low because the corpus is limited to CoS material and does not allow evaluation of the broader arc.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Within this corpus, Neville's most visible relationship is with the broader Gryffindor dormitory group (Harry, Ron, Dean, Seamus). He is a peer, not a close friend — present, occasionally helpful, occasionally a victim. His relationship with his Gran is referenced (the Howler anecdote) and implies a dynamic of fear and obligation. His relationship with Harry is one of mild solidarity and shared danger.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the dormitory peer dynamic. The Gran reference is maintained in the Howler scene. The relationship with Harry is preserved in the dormitory alert scene. No relationship is substantially altered.
key_relationships.difference: Minor compression. The book's recurring use of Neville as a dormitory presence (multiple scenes of him sleeping, snoring, being present) is reduced, but the emotional dynamics of his relationships are not changed.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: No significant relationship dynamic is altered. Small deduction for compression of the dormitory presence texture.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book corpus gives Neville several layers beyond simple comic relief: his specific fear of being 'almost a Squib', his talisman-buying as an independent (if misguided) response to danger, his self-aware articulation of vulnerability, his wand-slipping incompetence as a recurring pattern, and his small moments of social initiative (advising Ron, alerting Harry). These details build a character who is not merely a victim but someone with a specific psychological profile around magical inadequacy.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film retains the comic victimhood (pixies, fainting) and the small advisory moments (Howler, dormitory alert). It adds the common room speech. It loses the talisman-buying, the 'almost a Squib' self-identification, and the wand-slipping pattern.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The loss of the 'almost a Squib' thread is the most significant nuance cut. It is the detail that distinguishes Neville's fear from generic cowardice and gives it a specific, character-defining root. The talisman-buying scene also showed independent (if ineffective) agency. These losses simplify him toward pure comic relief.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The simplification is noticeable but not severe. The remaining material is faithful and the 'Why is it always me?' line preserves some self-aware texture. The character is not reduced to a single trait, but important nuance around magical inadequacy is missing.

lost_or_transferred_material: Neville buying protective talismans (onion, crystal, newt tail) out of fear of the Chamber attacks — an independent action revealing his specific anxiety
Neville's self-identification as 'almost a Squib' and the specific fear of magical inadequacy this represents
Neville's wand slipping in McGonagall's class (recurring incompetence pattern)
Neville's tongue-poking confusion over subject choices (minor texture of his academic anxiety)
Neville stuffing his fingers in his ears before the Howler opens (small physical comedy beat present in book, absent or reduced in film)
key_observations: The film portrayal of Neville Longbottom in Chamber of Secrets is largely faithful within the constraints of his minor role. Core traits — timidity, accident-proneness, occasional social initiative, comic victimhood — are preserved with specific evidence (Howler advice, fainting at Mandrake, pixie scene, dormitory alert, 'Why is it always me?'). The most meaningful loss is the 'almost a Squib' thread, which in the books gives his fear a specific psychological root distinguishing it from generic cowardice. The talisman-buying scene is also cut, removing his only independent action in this corpus. These losses simplify him toward pure comic relief without fully eliminating his character texture. Confidence is medium overall due to the limited and episodic nature of the corpus — Neville is a background character in CoS and the available material, while sufficient for the scenes shown, does not allow deep evaluation of motivation or arc.
